## Key Quotes

**"I have yet to meet one person that is at a reasonably high level in tech that has set up their own Zapier automations that are more than just a chain or two complicated."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Illustrating the gap between the promise of automation tools and actual adoption. Despite universal access and years of availability, even sophisticated users fail to build real workflows — pointing to a deeper structural problem, not a feature gap.

**"You're the first person I met who actually is functionally automating things."**
— Emre Sarbak
*Context:* After discussing how everyone talks about automation but nobody does it. A candid admission from a technically capable founder that real personal automation remains extraordinarily rare, validating V's differentiated position as a genuine power user.

**"People don't even have the primitives for making the transition between a deterministic, non-technical world mental model to a stochastic, sort of like technical model. It flips them on two different axes that leads people to be entirely paralyzed."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Articulating the core cognitive barrier to AI adoption. Not a skills problem or a tools problem — a fundamental mental model problem where people lack the conceptual vocabulary to even begin. This is a thesis-level insight that connects V's coaching background to the AI adoption challenge.

**"Once you know exactly what you want to do, the execution is going to be trivial. The hard part is to figure out what you want to do."**
— Emre Sarbak
*Context:* Laying out Clarion's core thesis on why conversational AI for decision support beats task automation. Execution commoditizes; the bottleneck is human sense-making in the face of conflicting information and competing priorities.

**"Workflows exist as a gestalt, as a very tightly integrated sort of interconnected set of systems, much like the human body. And so because Zapier and n8n force you into one workflow, it is definitionally only that useful."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Explaining why single-chain automation tools fail. The train-from-NYC-to-DC metaphor that followed landed the point: real productivity requires interconnected systems, not isolated tracks. This is an argument for platforms like Zo over point solutions.

**"I can manipulate them. It's fine. Because the goal for them is to talk."**
— Emre Sarbak
*Context:* Describing how he engineered AI character backstories (a female American in Berlin who attended a specific Turkish soccer game) to trigger emotional engagement in language learners. A revealing statement about ethical design — he sees engagement engineering as justified when it serves the user's actual goal. Speaks to deep UX instinct.

**"If you can make things — let's say you're a lawyer but you can use AI really well — now you're gonna make 10x or 100x. And then the lawyer that isn't doing that stuff, now he's got competition with 100 agents."**
— Emre Sarbak
*Context:* Describing the K-shaped divergence thesis — AI creating a superstar economy where the top performers multiply their output while everyone else gets compressed. Both agreed this is the structural reality, not a theoretical concern.

**"It effectively creates a hyper-powered petty bourgeoisie."**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Context:* Naming the societal risk of AI-powered professional stratification. The insight that a new class of "highly agentified 0.1% professionals" will emerge with "basically athlete-style representation" connects directly to the Careerspan vision and V's thinking about who serves this emerging tier.

## Strategic Questions Raised

**"Do you have a thesis for why people are still so overwhelmed — given we all have access to the same LLMs, the same productivity tools, the same agentic stuff?"**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* The foundational question that unlocked the most substantive part of the conversation. Both founders have built products that require users to make a cognitive leap — and both are encountering the same wall. This question reframed the discussion from "what are you building" to "why can't people use what already exists."

**"Can we create something that makes us better thinkers? AI as a cognitive harness?"**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* V's articulation of the next-startup vision: keeping lower-agency workers competitive by augmenting their thinking, not just their task execution. Argues these people bring pro-social qualities (empathy, interpersonal skills) that ultra-high-agency types often lack. This is where V's coaching instinct meets his product instinct.

**"Have you considered any other points of capturing that central understanding of who someone is? Beyond conversation?"**
— Vrijen Attawar
*Significance:* Probing whether Emre's approach is conversation-only or includes existing data sources. Reveals V's strategic framing: Bucket 1 (tap existing data) vs. Bucket 2 (engage and extract). Careerspan believes in Bucket 2, but V is testing whether Emre has a hybrid angle that could be complementary.
